title: Chapter 4: The House That Bites Back
content: Kael found the house on the third day.

It was a reinforced apartment on the edge of the Ruins — one of those stubborn holdouts where humans still lived despite the Bleeding, despite the vampire patrols, despite every incentive to flee to the fortified enclaves in the south. Salt lined the doorways. Silver crosses were nailed into the window frames. The door itself was heavy iron, reinforced with steel bars that had clearly been salvaged from a collapsed building.
Someone inside was very determined not to die.
He waited until dusk. The old woman — she had to be in her seventies, her face weathered by decades of Ruins survival — opened her door to shake out a rug, and Kael stepped into the streetlight.
"Who's there?" Her voice was sharp, suspicious. Her hand went to a silver amulet around her neck before Kael could even speak.
He met her eyes.

The telepathic suggestion was still new to him. Fledgling-level, weak, unreliable. He'd practiced on rats in the salt mines, then on Sera — with her permission, because she said he needed to learn. It wasn't mind control. It was more like... *smoothing*. Taking the edges off someone's suspicion. Making them feel, just for a moment, that the stranger in front of them wasn't a threat.

"I'm lost," Kael said. The suggestion pulsed gently behind his words. "I need shelter. Just for tonight. I can pay." He held up a handful of silver coins — Sera's emergency stash, scavenged from the cache — and watched the old woman's eyes flick from his face to the money and back again.
"Silver Dawn?" she asked.
"Former." Not a lie. He'd been a scout. He'd worn the silver. He'd killed vampires — four of them, before the fifth one had killed him back. "I work alone now."
The old woman studied him for a long moment. Her survival instinct warred with the suggestion, with the silver coins, with the simple human need for company after years of solitude.
"Just for tonight," she said finally. "You stay in the front room. Don't touch anything. And if you try anything—"
"I won't."
She opened the door.
Kael stepped across the threshold, and the invitation took hold.

He felt it physically — a subtle shift in the air, like the apartment itself had drawn a breath and decided to accept him. The salt stopped stinging. The crosses stopped humming with hostile energy. The space became *his* in a way that transcended property law. This was his territory now. His safe ground. Any vampire who tried to enter without their own explicit invitation would find their powers halved, their strength crippled, their connection to their own domain severed.
It was, Kael thought, a beautiful piece of architecture. A law designed to protect humans that could be weaponized to destroy vampires.
He waited until the old woman was asleep. Then he got to work.
The garlic went in first.

Crushed cloves pressed into every door frame, garlic water painted across every windowsill, dried garlic powder scattered across every threshold. The smell was overwhelming — Kael's new senses amplified it to the point of nausea — but that was the point. A vampire trying to force entry would be fighting through a wall of burning agony before they even crossed the doorway. Their eyes would water. Their skin would smoke. Their focus would shatter.
Next came the stakes.

Kael had spent the previous day in the salt mines, sharpening every piece of wood he could find. Chair legs. Broken shelving. A broom handle Sera had been saving for no reason she could articulate. He'd made twenty-two stakes in total, each one sharp enough to draw blood from his own palm just by touching the tip. He positioned them at ankle height behind every door, every window — not to kill, but to cripple. A wounded vampire was a slow vampire. A slow vampire was a dead vampire.
The silver crossbow Sera had insisted he bring went on a shelf by the window. Loaded. Ready. He doubted he'd need it.
The sun, after all, was a better weapon than silver.
Dawn came.

Kael stood at the window, watching the sky lighten from black to grey to pale gold. The clock in his head reached zero and kept going — no burn, no pain, no smoke. Just warmth. Real warmth. The warmth of a sun he'd thought he'd never feel again.

The old woman found him there an hour later. She'd woken to the smell of garlic and the sight of a stranger standing in full sunlight, and she'd frozen in her bedroom doorway with a silver knife in her trembling hand.
"You're a vampire," she whispered.
"Yes."
"You're standing in sunlight."
"Yes."
"That's not possible."
Kael turned from the window. "A lot of things aren't supposed to be possible. That doesn't stop them from happening."
She stared at him — at his pale skin, his sharpened features, the faint luminescence of his eyes in the dim interior light. The silver knife trembled. Then, very slowly, she lowered it.
"Why are you here?" she asked. "Why my house?"

"Because I need to kill another vampire. A Baron. He's been hunting me since the night I was turned. If I fight him in the open, I lose. But if I fight him here — in a home where I've been invited and he hasn't — I have a chance."
"And me?"
"You'll be safe. Stay in your bedroom. Don't open the door until you hear the screaming stop."

The old woman was quiet for a long moment. Outside, the sun climbed higher, painting the Ruins in shades of gold and shadow. The garlic smell was so strong now that Kael's eyes were watering, but he didn't dare remove any of it. He needed every advantage.

"You're different," she said finally. "From the others. I've seen vampires before — the ones who come at night, who knock on doors and try to charm their way inside. They all have the same look. Hungry. Cruel. Empty." She tilted her head, studying him. "You have a different look."
"What look?"
"Angry. But not at me." She tucked the silver knife into her belt. "Good luck, vampire. I hope you kill him."
She retreated to her bedroom and closed the door.
Kael turned back to the window. Somewhere in the Ruins, Baron Vereck's blood hounds were tracking his scent. The trap was set. The bait was waiting.
And the sun was rising.
